---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size45[
+ What should government do? "Deciding How to Decide"

+ Introducing the basic economics of pollution
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Bring the Munger book to class

<br>

.size15[Munger, M. C. (2000). Deciding How to Decide: "Experts," "The People," and "The Market." In *Analyzing Policy: Choices, Conflicts and Practices* (pp. 30–53). W.W. Norton & Company.]

<br>

Seminar style, circle the chairs! *If possible...*



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# The Semester: Three Sections 

.size45[
1. **How do we approach environmental politics? (4 wks)**

2. What are the complicating factors in environmental policymaking? (5 wks)

3. Designing an environmental policy (6 wks)
]

???

Remember, our semester is organized in three sections.

This week we continue to lay the foundations we need to engage with environmental politics in a productive way.

Let's talk about how we started to set the proverbial table last week.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# The Semester: Three Sections 

.size45[
1. **How do we approach environmental politics? (4 wks)**

    + "Politics"
]

???

We started by using a simulation to help us flesh out a working definition of politics.

### What is our operating definition of "politics" in this class?
+ (Rooted in terms of societal actors, distributions and rule-making)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# The Semester: Three Sections 

.size45[
1. **How do we approach environmental politics? (4 wks)**

    + "Politics"
    + "Environment"
]

???

On Friday we examined an argument by William Cronon about the dangers of wilderness in order to get us thinking about how we define "environmental".

<br>

### What advice did we take from the Cronon piece that will help us solve environmental problems?
+ (Cronon wants us to keep in mind that all stakeholders likely define the key concept differently)

+ We must recognize those differences and either work to align them or build policies that are compatible with the differences.

<br>

### And, at this point, what is your operating definition of "environmental"?

+ (Good question...)

<br>

Frankly, I'm not sure I have a good one.

Or if I do, it's personal.

Part of trying to solve problems using policy means having to embrace flexibility in terms of what the world is and what you want.

<br>

Not bad from only two days of work so far!




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

# The Semester: Three Sections 

.size45[
1. **How do we approach environmental politics? (4 wks)**

    + "Politics"
    + "Environment"
    + Today: "Policy"
]

???

Today we define policy!

### When you hear the word "policy" you immediately think of what?

+ (Government, right?)
    + Something expensive that government does or pays for?

+ (Or maybe the rules your employer has for you at work?)





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size60[**Policy Defined**]

.size40[
"A definite course or method of action selected (as by a government, institution, group or individual) from among alternatives and in the light of given conditions to guide and usually determine present and future decisions..." (Webster's Third International Dictionary).
]

???

### If I tell you that in this class you will each be acting as policy designers, per this definition what specific things am I expecting you to do when tackling a problem?

1. (You will propose a "course" or "method" of action that DETERMINES actor decisions)
    - Sounds like it will have to be very specific
    
2. (You will have to explicitly consider alternatives to your "course of action")

3. (You will have to adapt your "course of action" to the important "conditions" of the problem)

<br>

### Make sense?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Murray-make-a-difference.jpg')
background-size: 45%
background-position: center
class: middle, slideblue

???

<br>

SUPER important point for us this term.

<br>

We're not playing around.
- This isn't an exercise in, "if I was president or king of the world I would do X".

<br>

Key to this definition of policy is recognizing that it doesn't only apply to government.

The "decider" can be anyone, or any group, willing to put in the work to design a plan that meets the needs of its stakeholders and the conditions on the ground.

<br>

This is why I will insist that everyone select a local environmental problem to target this semester.

I want us tied to a specific community, in a specific moment in time and at a level where we can find concrete ways to make a difference.

<br>

### Fair enough?

<br>

Now that we've talked "policy" in incredibly broad strokes, let me show you how I've tied your assignments to that defintion.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-green_blue_swirl_side.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle, center

.size80[**Paper 1**]

.size60[
Write a report introducing us to the specific, local environmental problem you intend to target this semester
]

???

We'll get more into the details for the paper in a few weeks.

For now, I want each of you to start actively looking for a local environmental problem to work on.

Don't leave this for week 4 when you need to start writing the first paper.

Have ideas for yourself before then.

### Questions on this?

<br>

Ok, on to today's topic.

Let's start today with the Munger chapter from his book *Analyzing Policy.*




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Burke_Quote.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

### Why does the chapter open with this quote? What is he trying to help us understand about policy?

+ Munger wants to frame the act of policy analysis as a search for wisdom
    + e.g. "Good" policy is "wise" policy.
    
+ Much of the chapter is then an exploration of how we define "wise" in terms of policy options.

<br>

### How is this similar or different from how we defined "politics" last week?

+ (Pretty similar, right?)

+ Burke acknowledges that government is not "god-given" or part of the "natural order", it is a device built by us to achieve specific aims

+ In our distribution game there was no government, only a group of people seeking to develop some rule that could solve a problem for them.
    + You tried to devise some rules to meet your wants

<br>

One of the key takeaways for much of this chapter, and our entire semester, is that policy is **ALWAYS a choice and that choices have consequences**.

Put another way, **all policies create winners and losers**, it is the policy designer's job to distribute them.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-get_out_of_jail_free.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

Think back to our game for a moment.

### What was the game I asked you to play?

#### - In other words, before anybody started proposing anything, what was the dilemma or problem facing your society?

+ (1. Need to distribute a finite resource)
+ (2. Must achieve consensus or get nothing)
+ (3. Unclear what the preferences were for your fellow game players)

<br>

### How well do you think you could identify what the other people in the room wanted?

#### - How hard was it to identify everyone's TRUE preferences in the moment?

+ (Very... How do you disentangle or deduce true feelings from manipulative tactics or genuine proposals?)

+ (ALSO, not everyone's preferences were defined in the same terms!)
    + Likely true that some people had preferences over the outcome: give me the card or give it to them...
    + Also likely true that some had preferences over the process: we should decide like this...
    + some wanted the noise to stop...

<br>

Putting all this together and you get chaos due to a lack of clarity in the actual problem.

You probably see from this why societies tend to rapidly replace deliberative or direct democracies with representative ones. 





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1.png')
background-size: 60%
background-position: center
class: middle, slideblue

???

So, that was the game itself, now let's talk about the "wisdom" you displayed when playing it.

### What is Munger talking about when he refers to these competing forms of "wisdom" (see Fig 2.1)?

+ Everyone agrees the "right" policy is the "wise" policy, but this Figure helps us acknowledge that there are competing ways to define "wise."

+ Also trying to keep himself rooted in the quote from Burke he uses to motivate the chapter.

<br>

In our game we heard and discussed a number of different arguments about how we could or should distribute the benefits.

Let's try to place the arguments you made in class into these categories of "wisdom."



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1-efficiency.png')
background-size: 60%
background-position: center
class: middle, slideblue

???

**Small Groups Discuss and the Report Back**

### What is an example of an argument about how to distribute the cards from our game that was all about promoting efficiency?

#### - Or what is an argument we could make on these grounds?

*ON BOARD*

+ (?)

##### chapter notes
Markets vs experts: How do we distribute the rewards to ensure no better distribution is possible? How can expert tweaks to the market reduce market failures?

+ Note that Munger defines "expert" as "someone who has the goal of improving the functioning of politics or markets" (31).

+ Market failures
    1. Monopolies (e.g. antitrust)
    2. Externalities (e.g. pollution)
    3. Under supply of public goods (e.g. free-riding)
    4. Information problems (e.g. drug safety)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1-equity.png')
background-size: 60%
background-position: center
class: middle, slideblue

???

**Small Groups Discuss and the Report Back**

### What is an example of an argument about how to distribute the cards in our game that was all about promoting equity?

#### - Or what is an argument we could make on these grounds?

*ON BOARD*

+ (?)

##### chapter notes
Disagreements about the outcomes of market processes
- e.g. Fairness

But there are two conflicting meanings of fairness too!
1. "Fair" as in "average"
2. "Fair" as in equitable



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1-reform.png')
background-size: 60%
background-position: center
class: middle, slideblue

???

**Small Groups Discuss and the Report Back**

### What is an example of an argument about how to distribute the cards in our game that was all about institutional reform?

#### - Or what is an argument we could make on these grounds?

*ON BOARD*

+ (?)
+ Class example: Charles's ominous 30 second countdown!

##### chapter notes
+ Can be either benign or evil (e.g. broaden the vote vs restrict it)
+ Often implicate the very functioning of your government or democracy


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1.png')
background-size: 60%
background-position: center
class: middle, slideblue

???

Ok, connect these threads for me.

This is a tough question but I'd like us to brainstorm.

Back to your groups!

### In what specific ways can we use this framework as advice for aspiring policy-makers and problem-solvers?

*ON BOARD*

- ?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-cavemen.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Let's now shift to one of my favorite thought experiments in any textbook ever.

### Who are Hun-gats and why does Munger spend so much time asking us to imagine a made-up tribe of cavemen starving to death?

+ (Literally a tribe of hunter-gatherers in pre-history)

+ (Thought experiment to help us think about the birth of policy.)

+ (Illustration of the "state of nature" so frequently referenced by olden philosophers like Hobbes)

<br>

### Out of curiosity, before our index card distribution game started, were you operating in a "state of nature"? Why or why not?

- (Nope!)
    - Munger seems to imply that a "state of nature" represents the absence of any market, expert or political "context" in a given situation (p35).
    - That was definitely not the case here (or probably anywhere, ever)!

<br>

### Ok, talk to me about the Hun-Gats. Why do they need policy? Why can't they live in a perfectly free, libertarian existence?

+ They are running out of food (or at least the food they know is safe to eat)
+ They can stay independent and "free" but not if they want to survive and thrive over time!

(SLIDE)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-2.png')
background-size: 75%
background-position: center

???

I think this is a super clever way to illustrate a bunch of important elements that are key to policy-making.

### What does Figure 2.2 make clear about the basic role of policy in any society?

+ (The desire to survive appears to make policy inevitable)

+ (Even the policies you don't, if targeted at a real problem, may reduce the risk to you of not surviving!)

+ ?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3.png')
background-size: 55%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

In Figure 2.3 Munger lays out his "three stages of policy choice."

Let's make sure we're all on the same page with this.

<br>

### How did the Hun-gats navigate these three stages when grappling with their hunger issues?

#### - What stood out for you in Munger's hypothetical narrative?

+ Battles over expertise are central to policy-making

+ Policy can only ever be as effective as the authority that compels the behavior
    + The birth of religious rules?

+ Any society with resource issues will deal with free-riding problems



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3-step1.png')
background-size: 55%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

In order to bring this more into the world we inhabit, let's zoom in on the stages.

**Small Groups Discuss and the Report Back**

### As the designer of a policy meant to address an environmental problem, what does Stage 1 mean for you?

+ (?)

<br>

### How does the Cronon piece from last week speak to Stage 1 of this process?

+ (Important to question our implicit assumptions, especially if we haven't examined them before.)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3-step2.png')
background-size: 55%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

So, we've convinced the community we have a problem to address.

**Small Groups Discuss and the Report Back**

### As the designer of a policy meant to address an environmental problem, what does Stage 2 mean for you?

+ (?)

<br>

### Under what conditions should we prefer a collective over a private decision for a given problem?

#### - In other words, what is the collective vs private trade-off? 

+ (?)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-3-step3.png')
background-size: 55%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

**Small Groups Discuss and the Report Back**

So, we've convinced the community we have a problem to address and that we need a collective decision.

### As the designer of a policy meant to address an environmental problem, what does Stage 3 mean for you?

+ (?)

<br>

### Under what conditions should we prefer a democratic choice over delegating to experts? Or vice-versa?

#### - In other words, what is the trade-off? 

+ (?)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-4.png')
background-size: 67%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

Ok, I want to make sure we don't blow past Figure 2.4 because this one carries a super important lesson for us about policy-making and the environment.

### What is the key, big picture observation for thinking about this 2x2 grid?

#### - How do we figure out where a specific issue is supposed to go in the grid?

+ (Affecting your welfare is INCREDIBLY subjective)
    + Anyone who tells you something is clearly about liberty or clearly about community harms is telling you about their personal values.
    
+ Figuring out an argument to move your opposition across boxes can be a big part of selling your policy

<br>

### Make sense?

<br>

### Questions on the Munger chapter or how we apply the material to your projects this semester?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-ideology_oversimplified.jpg')
background-size: 85%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

Quick aside, and feel free to completely disagree with me here!

I tend to think that training as a policy analyst is an excellent counter to the ideological nonsense that seems to organize much of our political discourse.

<br>

If your goal is to treat democracy like a team sport, then by all means, pick a team and ride or die!

- Liberal, conservative, libertarian, marxist, whatever

<br>

If your goal is to solve actual problems facing a real community then an effective policy designer must be flexible and empathetic to their needs.

That kind of flexibility is somewhat ntithetical to the one-size-fits-all version of political identity held by many.

We live in a complex and diverse society filled with lots of different people living in very close quarters.

Our job is to figure out where they are, what they want and how we can help them solve problems using policies that EVERYONE will accept and obey over time.

<br>

Long story short, I believe our democracy would be far healthier if we all had a little more policy analyst in us!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size45[
OpenStax. (2016). 12.1 The Economics of Pollution. In *Principles of Economics*.
]

.size45[
+ Market failure
+ Externality / Spillover / Social Costs
  + Negative externality
  + Positive externality
]

???

Let's jump to the environmental econ chapter.

I gave you this as a crash course in the economic language that is vital to understanding the discussions surrounding most environmental problems at the policy level.

<br>

### Any questions on the concepts or techniques introduced in the chapter?

<br>

### Do these ideas look anything like what we've been dealing with in the Munger chapter?

(SLIDE)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-1-Munger_Fig2-1.png')
background-size: 60%
background-position: right
class: middle, slidegreen

.left[
.size45[
Market failures

Spillovers

Social Costs

Externalities
+ Negative
+ Positive
]]

???

### Where do these issues place us in Munger's Figure 2.1 about the kinds of policies we need in the environmental arena?



<br>

## Chapter Notes from Glossary

additional external cost
    additional costs incurred by third parties outside the production process when a unit of output is produced

externality
    a market exchange that affects a third party who is outside or “external” to the exchange; sometimes called a “spillover”

market failure
    When the market on its own does not allocate resources efficiently in a way that balances social costs and benefits; externalities are one example of a market failure

negative externality
    a situation where a third party, outside the transaction, suffers from a market transaction by others

positive externality
    a situation where a third party, outside the transaction, benefits from a market transaction by others 

social costs
    costs that include both the private costs incurred by firms and also additional costs incurred by third parties outside the production process, like costs of pollution

spillover
    see externality 


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle, center, inverse

.size80[**For Thursday**]

.size70[
What are the key elements of a domestic process that aims to solve environmental problems?
]

???

### Questions?
